region_of_origin: Fujian (Hokkien) and Guangdong, China; established as a Singapore Chinese household staple
food_subgroup: Slow-braised pork belly in dark soy sauce with rock sugar, star anise, garlic, and hard-boiled eggs

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Tau Yew Bak (Hokkien-style braised pork belly in dark soy sauce). Closest parent is braised pork belly in soy sauce. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Braised pork belly — closest FoodEx2 parent for Hokkien-style tau yew bak (dark soy sauce, rock sugar, five-spice preparation); match type is closest_parent
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for tau yew bak. A03LX (pig meat products) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for braised pork belly in dark soy sauce.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200g, comprising roughly 150g of braised pork belly and 50g of braising sauce, served without rice.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Pork Belly with Dark Soy (Tau Yew Bak) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tau Yew Bak / braised pork belly with dark soy sauce. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (pork belly, braised, closest match) where HPB data was not available, as documented in per_100g_meta.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Pork Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database (Pork-based braised dishes with sugar and soy sauce)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tau Yew Bak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with braised pork dishes containing soy sauce and sugar. The high fat and protein content of pork belly substantially attenuates postprandial glucose response. Rock sugar contributes modest carbohydrate (sucrose GI ~65) but is diluted by the dominant fat and protein matrix. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 13.0) / 100 = 5.85, rounded to 5.9. Carbohydrate per 200g serving = 6.5 × 2 = 13.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (520 mg/100g) from dark soy sauce and light soy sauce absorbed into the braising liquid and pork
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit tau yew bak to a maximum of 50–80g per occasion (approximately 260–416mg sodium) and must avoid drizzling additional braising sauce; a standard 200g serving delivers 1040mg sodium, exceeding half the WHO daily recommended maximum of 2000mg from a single dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic load per serving (GL ~5.9) due to dominant fat and protein matrix suppressing postprandial glucose; however, high saturated fat content raises insulin resistance risk with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tau yew bak has a low glycemic load and does not acutely spike blood glucose when eaten alone, but the very high saturated fat content (8.2g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance over time; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 100g and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice to minimise overall glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (8.2g/100g), high cholesterol (92mg/100g), high sodium (520mg/100g), and trans fat (0.1g/100g) — a combination that adversely affects LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, and endothelial function
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tau yew bak is a high-risk dish for patients with cardiovascular disease due to its combined burden of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and sodium; consumption should be restricted to small portions (80–100g) on infrequent occasions, with preference for lean meat layers over the fat and skin, and the braising sauce should be avoided entirely.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (8.2g/100g) from pork belly fat and skin is proinflammatory; a 200g serving delivers 16.4g saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit tau yew bak to small portions (80–100g) on infrequent occasions, choosing lean meat layers and avoiding the thick fat and skin; the high thiamin (0.42mg/100g) and selenium (22mcg/100g) content provide some anti-inflammatory micronutrient support but are insufficient to offset the saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tau Yew Bak (豆油肉) is a quintessential Hokkien-style braised pork belly dish that is one of the most beloved comfort foods in Singapore Chinese home cooking and kopitiam culture. Thick slabs of pork belly — comprising alternating layers of lean meat, fat, and skin — are slow-braised for 1.5 to 2 hours in a deeply aromatic liquid of dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, rock sugar, star anise, and garlic until the pork skin becomes gelatinous and trembling, the fat layers melt to silky tenderness, and the lean meat absorbs the sweet-savoury braising liquid to a rich mahogany colour. Hard-boiled eggs are typically added to the pot and take on the same deep colour and flavour. The dish is served with steamed white rice and a generous drizzle of the reduced braising sauce. It is closely related to the Chinese red-braised pork (hong shao rou / 红烧肉) of Hunan and Zhejiang cuisines, but the Singapore Hokkien version is more soy-forward and less sweet, and is often associated with the Dongpo Rou (东坡肉) tradition. Nutritionally, tau yew bak is energy-dense at 295 kcal/100g, very high in fat (22.5g/100g) and saturated fat (8.2g/100g), high in sodium (520mg/100g), and moderate in protein (14.5g/100g). It is an excellent source of thiamin B1 (0.42mg/100g) and selenium (22mcg/100g) from the pork. Clinically, it is a high-concern dish for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat burden, and should be consumed in small portions by individuals managing these conditions.
common_user_queries: is tau yew bak good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central braised pork belly data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, reducing confidence for the GI component only.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for this preparation — pork belly and soy-based braising liquid contain no dietary fibre; this is naturally zero. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for tau yew bak (braised pork belly in dark soy sauce) is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central braised pork belly entry as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this braised preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database. Copper (0.08mg/100g), manganese (0.05mg/100g), and vitamin K (1.2mcg/100g) values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (pork belly, braised, NDB No. 10006 / closest match) where HPB data was unavailable. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tau yew bak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 200g serving = (45 × 13.0) / 100 = 5.9. Portions list in input contained two duplicate 250g entries; deduplicated to one default 200g serving (HPB-verified) for the output. The dish is not halal as it contains pork.

Set water_g to -1 with documentation in notes.
